title: Anger at the Healthcare System During Pregnancy
dek: *The healthcare system often fails pregnant people at the exact moment they need it most. When your body is changing faster than anyone can track and your questions meet hurried dismissals, anger becomes not just understandable but necessary. Here's how to recognize it, honor it, and use it to advocate for the care you deserve.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Pregnancy magnifies systemic healthcare failures, turning normal advocacy into a minefield of dismissed concerns and rushed appointments. Your anger at being unheard is both rational and protective, signaling that something in the care relationship needs repair. Learning to work with this anger rather than suppress it can improve outcomes for you and your baby.

She sat in the waiting room for forty-seven minutes past her appointment time, watching the clock because she'd already rearranged her entire afternoon to be here. When the nurse finally called her name, the exam lasted nine minutes. Blood pressure, fundal height, Doppler on the belly, a few questions answered while her provider typed notes without meeting her eyes. She left with a pamphlet about sleep positions and a feeling she couldn't quite name, something that sat hot and hard beneath her ribs but never made it to her face. ## The Particular Betrayal of Pregnancy Care Healthcare fails people in a thousand quiet ways, but pregnancy concentrates those failures into a season when you're already navigating enormous physical and emotional transformation. You come to appointments with legitimate questions about pain, bleeding, anxiety, or the strange pulling sensation in your pelvis, and you're met with reassurances so generic they couldn't possibly apply to your specific body. The five-minute visit becomes standard. Your concerns get filed under "normal pregnancy discomfort" before you've finished describing them. In our experience, this dismissal lands differently when you're pregnant because the stakes include another life. You're not just advocating for yourself anymore. You're trying to protect someone who can't speak, can't complain, can't tell anyone if something's wrong. And when the system treats your vigilance as neurotic overconcern, when providers smile indulgently at your written list of questions, the anger that rises isn't selfishness. It's an ancient protective response doing exactly what it evolved to do. Robert Augustus Masters writes about anger as a boundary-setting emotion, one that arises when something precious is being violated or threatened. In pregnancy, that "something precious" is both your bodily autonomy and your baby's wellbeing. The healthcare system asks you to surrender enormous amounts of control, to accept interventions and protocols and scheduling constraints, often without meaningful explanation or room for questions. When that surrender doesn't come with corresponding care and attention, anger is the rational response. ## When Your Body Knows Before Your Mind Does You might not recognize the anger at first. It shows up as tension in your jaw during appointments, as the way you hold your breath when the medical assistant rushes through intake questions, as the tightness across your shoulders that doesn't release even after you've left the building. Your nervous system registers the threat, the being-unheard, the systematic diminishment, long before your conscious mind names it as anger. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body teaches us that the nervous system doesn't distinguish between physical threats and relational ones. When you're in a medical setting where you feel unseen or unheard, your body responds with the same activation patterns it would use facing any danger: elevated heart rate, shallow breathing, muscle tension preparing for fight or flight. Except you can't fight your obstetrician, and you can't flee the prenatal appointment you need. So the activation gets trapped, cycling through your system with nowhere to go. Pay attention to what your body does in medical spaces. Does your throat tighten when you try to ask questions? Do you find yourself apologizing before you've even stated your concern? Do you leave appointments feeling vaguely defeated but tell yourself you're being "too sensitive"? These are signs that anger is present but suppressed, working underground because you've learned it's not safe or acceptable to express. The suppression itself causes problems. The NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system documents how chronic anger suppression correlates with elevated blood pressure, increased cortisol, and inflammatory markers that affect both maternal and fetal health. Your body keeps the score even when your mind tries to be reasonable. ## The Gendered Dimension Nobody Mentions Soraya Chemaly's research on women and anger illuminates a specific dynamic at play in pregnancy care: women are socialized from childhood to suppress anger, to prioritize others' comfort over their own needs, to be pleasant and accommodating even when being harmed. Pregnancy intensifies this conditioning. You're supposed to be glowing, grateful, focused on the miracle of life. Anger doesn't fit the script. Medical providers, consciously or not, often reinforce this. The "difficult patient" label gets applied quickly to pregnant people who ask too many questions, who push back on protocols, who insist their pain is real and significant. And because you need ongoing care, because you'll see these same providers through delivery and postpartum, there's enormous pressure to stay on their good side. So you smile. You say "okay" when you mean "I don't understand" or "that doesn't feel right." You perform gratitude for care that isn't actually caring. This performance has costs. Over the years I've seen how unexpressed anger during pregnancy often resurfaces in postpartum rage, in the sudden explosions that feel out of proportion to the trigger but are actually the accumulated weight of months of swallowed protest. Or it turns inward, becoming depression, becoming a sense that you're failing at pregnancy because you can't just relax and trust the process like everyone tells you to. ## What the Anger Is Protecting Strip away the layers of should and supposed-to, and most anger at the healthcare system during pregnancy is protecting something specific: your right to be known as an individual, your need for information that helps you make real choices, your expectation that care providers will actually care. These aren't unreasonable demands. They're the bare minimum of ethical medical practice. When your provider doesn't remember basic facts about your history from one appointment to the next, when test results take weeks to communicate, when you're told to "just trust us" instead of being given data you can evaluate, when your pain is attributed to anxiety without investigation, your anger is pointing at genuine failures. It's not a character flaw or a hormone surge. It's appropriate outrage at being treated as a vessel rather than a person. The challenge is learning to work with the anger rather than against it. This means first acknowledging it exists, which for many people requires slowing down enough to feel what's happening in the body. Find a quiet moment and place your hand on your chest. Take three slow breaths. Ask yourself: what am I actually angry about? Not the philosophical answer, but the specific, concrete thing. Maybe it's that your calls don't get returned. Maybe it's that you've asked about your family history of preeclampsia three times and still haven't gotten a clear answer about monitoring protocols. Maybe it's that every appointment feels like you're bothering someone. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Working With Healthcare Anger You can do this anywhere, though privacy helps. Sit or stand comfortably. Bring to mind a specific moment of anger with your healthcare provider or the system. Don't try to change or fix the feeling. Just locate it in your body. Where do you feel it? Chest, throat, belly, hands? Place your hand on that spot. Breathe normally. Say out loud or silently: "This anger is protecting something I care about." Feel the truth of that. Then ask: "What does this anger need me to know?" Wait for whatever arises. It might be a word, an image, a body sensation, a sudden clarity about what you need to say or do. Don't force it. If nothing comes immediately, that's fine. The practice is in creating space for the anger to be felt and acknowledged rather than pushed away. Do this for three to five minutes. When you're ready to close, take one deep breath and thank your anger for showing up. This isn't sarcastic. Your anger is working on your behalf, trying to protect you and your baby from inadequate care. ## Practical Advocacy When You're Already Exhausted Acknowledging anger is one thing. Using it to actually change your care situation is another, especially when you're already managing fatigue, nausea, body changes, work demands, and the mental load of preparing for a baby. The healthcare system counts on your exhaustion. It counts on you being too tired to push back, too overwhelmed to switch providers, too uncertain to trust your own judgment. Start small. Before your next appointment, write down your three most important questions or concerns. Not ten, not everything you've been worrying about. Three. Bring the list. Tell your provider at the start: "I have three things I need to discuss today." This frames the visit around your priorities rather than defaulting to their agenda. If you get rushed or dismissed, practice saying: "I need you to slow down. This is important to me." You don't have to be angry in your delivery. Calm, clear, firm works just as well and sometimes better. But you do have to advocate, which means speaking up even when it feels uncomfortable. Consider bringing support to appointments. A partner, friend, or doula can witness interactions, take notes, and help you remember what was said. They can also ask the questions you forget or feel too intimidated to voice. There's power in not being alone in the room. If you're consistently feeling unheard, it might be time to change providers. This feels impossible when you're already established in a practice, when you're past the first trimester, when your insurance limits options. But staying with a provider who doesn't respect you has consequences that ripple through your entire pregnancy and birth. The assessments hub includes questions that can help you evaluate whether your current care relationship is actually working. ## The Body Keeps Speaking Your body during pregnancy is already a chorus of new sensations, some pleasant, most just strange. Learning to distinguish between normal pregnancy changes and signs that something's wrong requires a healthcare provider who listens when you describe what you're experiencing. When that listening is absent, you're left trying to interpret signals without reliable guidance. Notice what happens in your body during and after medical appointments. Do you feel lighter, reassured, equipped with information? Or do you feel tense, confused, vaguely anxious? Your nervous system's response tells you something about the quality of care you're receiving. If you consistently leave appointments feeling worse than when you arrived, that's information worth heeding. The fire toolkit offers practices for working with the physical activation that anger creates. When you're pregnant, some traditional anger-release methods aren't appropriate, but you can still move the energy. Gentle walking while letting yourself mentally rehearse what you wish you'd said. Writing unsent letters to your provider detailing everything you're angry about. Talking to someone who won't try to fix or minimize your feelings but will simply witness them. At night, when sleep is already complicated by the mechanics of a changing body, anger can keep you wired and restless. Creating a wind-down routine that includes the nervous system can help. Some people find that [Pukka Night Time Herbal Tea (20 bags)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0030EGQ6K?tag=spankyspinola-20) supports relaxation, though you'll want to check any herbal supplement with your provider given pregnancy considerations. The ritual of preparation can be as important as the tea itself, a moment of tending to yourself in a season when everyone else seems focused on the baby. ## Questions People Actually Ask ### Is it normal to feel angry at my doctor during pregnancy? Yes, completely normal and often justified. Pregnancy doesn't obligate you to be endlessly grateful for substandard care. If your provider is dismissive, rushed, or fails to address your concerns, anger is an appropriate response. The question isn't whether the anger is normal but whether the care you're receiving meets basic standards of respect and attention. ### How do I know if I should switch providers or if I'm just being hormonal? Trust your gut, but also look at patterns. One frustrating appointment might be a bad day. Consistent experiences of being unheard, having concerns dismissed, feeling rushed, or leaving with unanswered questions suggest a real problem with the care relationship. "Being hormonal" is often used to dismiss legitimate concerns. Your feelings are data, especially during pregnancy when you're more attuned to threat and safety signals. ### Can anger during pregnancy hurt my baby? Chronic, unprocessed anger that keeps your stress response activated can affect both you and your developing baby. But the anger itself isn't the problem. The suppression of anger, the ongoing situation that's causing it, and the lack of resolution are what create sustained stress. Working with your anger, using it as information to improve your care situation, is protective rather than harmful. Supporting your nervous system through this process matters. Some people find [NOW L-Tryptophan 500 mg (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0019LWV56?tag=spankyspinola-20) helpful for mood support, though always consult your provider before adding supplements during pregnancy. ## Building Different Relationships Not all healthcare providers operate the same way. Some truly listen. Some ask open-ended questions and wait for your answers. Some communicate test results promptly and explain what they mean in language you can understand. Some acknowledge when they don't know something and commit to finding out. These providers exist, though the system doesn't always make it easy for them to practice this way either. If you're stuck with inadequate care because of insurance, location, or timing, you can still build support around the gaps. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return often to the idea that healing happens in relationship, that no single person has to meet all your needs. A good therapist, a knowledgeable doula, a prenatal yoga teacher who creates space for real questions, an online community of people navigating similar struggles, your own research into evidence-based practices, all these can supplement what your medical provider isn't offering. Our herbal cabinet includes information on plants traditionally used to support emotional wellbeing during pregnancy, always with the caveat that you're the expert on your own body and your own situation. [Yogi Relaxed Mind (96 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00404XCLQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) is one option some people explore, though pregnancy changes how your body processes everything, so what worked before might not work now. The anger you feel isn't asking you to become a different person, more assertive or confident or articulate than you naturally are. It's asking you to take yourself seriously, to treat your concerns as legitimate, to expect basic respect from the people charged with your care. That's not too much to ask. It's the foundation of ethical medicine. You'll find additional perspectives on anger and embodiment at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the focus is on spiritual dimensions of difficult emotions. But you don't need a spiritual framework to honor what you're feeling. You just need to recognize that your anger, however inconvenient or uncomfortable, is on your side. It's trying to protect you. Listen to it. Let it inform your choices. And know that demanding better care isn't being difficult. It's being responsible to yourself and the life you're growing. ## Keep reading - [Sandwich Generation Anger: The Math Doesn't Work](/article/sandwich-generation-anger-the-math-doesnt-work) - [Anger at Other Parents at the Playground](/article/anger-at-other-parents-at-the-playground) - [Anger When Your Child Is the One Bullying](/article/anger-when-your-child-is-the-one-bullying) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on anger at the healthcare system during pregnancy, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
